farLeftText: From 1h ago 15.58 EDT Darline Graham says she will run for a full Senate term Darline Graham, the South Carolina interim senator and sister of the late Lindsey Graham, said she plans to run for a full Senate term. In an interview with Sean Hannity that will air in full on Monday, Graham – who was appointed by Governor Henry McMaster to finish the remainder of her brother’s term – said she had “made a decision”. “I’m in,” she told the Fox News anchor. Graham, 62, made history as the first woman to represent South Carolina in the Senate when she was sworn in last week. Her brother, Lindsey died earlier this month, aged 71, after what his office called a “brief and sudden illness”. The chief medical examiner then preliminarily ruled that he died of aortic dissection due to cardiovascular disease. On Friday, Trump said he had encouraged Darline Graham to run for a full six-year term representing South Carolina. “I asked Darline, for the Good of our Nation, to run for the U.S. Senate in the Special Republican Primary,” he wrote on Truth Social. “Darline, who comes from an absolutely incredible family, has been a WINNER all of her life and, should she accept, has my Complete and Total Endorsement.” Since Lindsey won the Republican nomination last month, the state will now hold a special primary to replace him on the ballot on 11 August. Share Updated at 16.01 EDT 42m ago 16.24 EDT Donald Trump signed an executive order on Monday that makes it harder for US defense contractors to obtain waivers that allow them to buy critical minerals from an “unreliable foreign supplier”, as part of an ongoing effort to decrease the importance of China to US defense supply chains. The new rules mean defense contractors like Lockheed Martin and Boeing seeking waivers to allow them to buy from a Chinese supplier will need to prove that they searched for alternatives, ⁠explain where their materials come from and lay out a plan to move ​away from a foreign-owned source. Currently waivers can be obtained by showing that a Chinese supplier is the easiest or cheapest option. “No more: ‘we tried ‌nothing and we’re out of options’,” the White House trade adviser and anti-China hawk Peter Navarro said at a briefing on the order, Reuters reports. Many critical ‌minerals and processed materials used in missiles, aircraft and other USA military systems still rely on Chinese suppliers, leaving companies caught between demands ​to move away from Beijing and the need to keep weapons flowing to US forces and allies. Share Updated at 16.01 EDT 1h ago 15.49 EDT Mike Johnson's ambitious legislative to-do list before August recess Chris Stein The Republican speaker Mike Johnson has laid out an ambitious to-do list for the House of Representatives this week, which is the last before lawmakers depart Washington DC for the lengthy August recess. Here’s a rundown of all the bills that the House may – and we truly mean may – vote on this week: A measure that would extend government funding until 4 December. Congress is supposed to pass 12 bills funding different parts of the federal government by the end of the fiscal year on 30 September, but does not appear set to reach that deadline this year, so House Republicans have proposed this bill to keep funding going until roughly a month after the midterms. The Stop Insider Trading act, which purports to prevent lawmakers from trading stocks while in office, a long-running complaint of lawmakers from both parties. However, ethics groups have criticized it as insufficient, while Republicans have inserted language imposing a nationwide voter ID requirement, ensuring Democrats will oppose this bill. The House’s version of the National Defense Authorization Act. This is seen as a must-pass bill that outlines defense policy for the fiscal year and authorizes $1.15tn in spending per Donald Trump’s request. The Senate has a separate version of this bill, but Democrats blocked it from advancing last week in protest of the ongoing war with Iran. Perhaps the biggest bill before the House this week is a budgetary blueprint that sets the stage for Republicans to write a party-line measure that is expected to, among other things, pay for the war with Iran. However, this measure faces an array of challenges, including from fiscal hawks who complain that its expected $95bn cost is not offset by any spending cuts, and other Republicans upset that it does not answer the Trump administration’s request for an extra $350bn in defense spending. We mentioned earlier that these measure may come up for a vote this week. That’s because House Republicans have a very slim margin of control and can afford to lose few votes on any bill, raising the possibility that Johnson is forced to delay consideration of some of these measures due to dissenters in his own ranks and opposition from Democrats. We will be watching to see how it shakes out. Share Updated at 16.02 EDT 1h ago 15.38 EDT Here's a recap of the day so far Troy Jackson – a logger, longtime legislator and former president of the Maine senate – is now poised to become the Democratic nominee in Maine’s US Senate race. Several contenders filed paperwork to be considered at a 25 July special convention of 601 delegates tasked with selecting a new nominee. But over the weekend, Jackson’s serious challengers dropped out one after another, clearing a swift and unobstructed path for him. Jackson, who lost the Democratic gubernatorial primary earlier this year, is now the presumptive choice. In a post on Truth Social, Donald Trump appeared to push back on New York mayor Zohran Mamdani’s comments to the New York Times that he was in “an active conversation” with the city’s legal department about their remit arrest Israeli prime minister Benjamin Netanyahu, when he attends the UN general assembly later this year. The president insisted that the Israeli leader “will not be arrested, in any way, shape, or form, while in the United States of America”. A federal judge has temporarily blocked the $111bn merger between Paramount Skydance and Warner Bros Discovery (WBD), after 12 Democratic state attorneys said that the deal violates antitrust law, and reduces competition in both the film and cable television industries. The states’ top prosecutors called on Warner and Paramount to not close the transaction until after a court had time to “fully evaluate” their claims. And when the companies refused, they filed for a temporary restraining order – which is what district judge Araceli Martínez-Olguín granted for two weeks on Monday. Trump has also spoken to the new British prime minister, Andy Burnham, according to a read-out from Downing Street. According to the statement, Burnham “underlined his commitment to defence and security” and said “ensuring the security of the UK and its allies was at the top of his agenda”. Trump updated the newly minted UK prime minister on the “The president then updated on the situation in the Middle East, and Burnham outlined the UK’s “commitment to securing the movement of shipping in the strait of Hormuz,” according to Downing Street. Tom Homan, the president’s so-called “border czar” told reporters that fatal shootings by Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) agents are due to the fact that those who were killed “failed to comply with law enforcement”. Homan added that “all they had to do is comply. If they did, they’d be alive today”. This comes after two men, both immigrants, were killed during separate encounters earlier in July with officers attempting to conduct traffic stops in Texas and Maine. The FBI is investigating an incident where an “incendiary device” that was deployed outside 26 Federal Plaza, a notorious Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) holding facility in New York. In a statement, FBI director Kash Patel said that “an individual is in custody and two minor injuries reported thus far”. The Joint Terrorism Task Force is also part of the investigation. Share 2h ago 15.21 EDT Federal judge temporarily blocks multibillion merger between Paramount and Warner Bros A federal judge has temporarily blocked the $111bn merger between Paramount Skydance and Warner Bros Discovery (WBD), after 12 Democratic state attorneys said that the deal violates antitrust law, and reduces competition in both the film and cable television industries. The states’ top prosecutors called on Warner and Paramount to not close the transaction until after a court had time to “fully evaluate” their claims. And when the companies refused, they filed for a temporary restraining order – which is what district judge Araceli Martínez-Olguín granted for two weeks on Monday. “This is a critical first win in our case to ensure this megamerger never sees the light of day,” Rob Bonta, the California attorney general, said in a statement following Monday’s order. “History tells the tale of what happens when a few people have great power over markets that are central to Americans’ lives: fewer opportunities for more people, worse products and services for all people.” Trump’s justice department already approved the merger last month. As my colleague Jeremy Barr reported at the time, the deal was approved by the department’s anti-trust division after months of review, and despite the concerns of many people in the entertainment and media industries who believe it will hurt competition by reducing the number of film studios and – most likely – merging two news networks, Paramount’s CBS News and CNN, owned by WBD. Share Updated at 15.33 EDT 2h ago 15.02 EDT Trump says Iran will 'pay' for killing US soldiers 'many times over' In a post on Truth Social earlier, Donald ⁠Trump said that Iran ⁠would “pay” ​for ‌killing ‌US soldiers “many times over” after three service members ‌were killed ​in ⁠action ​over the ​weekend. In another social media post, the president appeared to push back on New York mayor Zohran Mamdani’s comments to the New York Times that he was in “an active conversation” with the city’s legal department about their remit arrest Israeli prime minister Benjamin Netanyahu, when he attends the UN general assembly later this year. “I believe that prime minister Netanyahu belongs in The Hague,” Mamdani said on The Interview podcast. “He’s a war criminal who has been charged by the international criminal court. “And what you will find is that is an opinion that is held by many, purely because of what his actions have wrought over these last many years,” the New York mayor added. However, Trump on Monday insisted that the Israeli leader “will not be arrested, in any way, shape, or form, while in the United States of America”. The president added: “He is fighting against the Islamic Republic of Iran, which recently killed 52,000 innocent protestors, and has spent the last 47 years killing American Soldiers, and others.” Share Updated at 15.20 EDT 2h ago 14.48 EDT Homan also responded to a report from the New York Times that federal agents were told the FBI would no longer investigate confrontations with immigration agents, according to unnamed sources people briefed on the decision. Trump’s border czar noted that assault on federal immigration officers are usually handled by Homeland Security Investigations (HSI), a division of the Department of Homeland Security (DHS). “The FBI does get involved with them, but we’re not in meetings,” Homan said. “But when it comes to shootings, FBI is always taking the lead. So I think that messaging is a little confusing … as far as I’m concerned, I’m not aware of any change in policy, and I think FBI and DHS actually pushed back on that story.” Share 3h ago 14.21 EDT Homan says fatal ICE shootings are down to people who 'failed to comply with law enforcement' Donald Trump’s so-called “border czar” Tom Homan spoke to reporters at the White House today, noting that fatal shootings by Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) agents are due to the fact that those who were killed “failed to comply with law enforcement”. “All they had to do is comply. If they did, they’d be alive today,” Homan said. View image in fullscreen The White House border czar, Tom Homan, speaks with reporters outside the White House on Monday. Photograph: Mark Schiefelbein/AP Most recently, Lorenzo Salgado Araujo and Joan Sebastián Durán Guerrero were killed during separate encounters earlier in July with officers attempting to conduct traffic stops in Texas and Maine. When it comes to body cameras, the border czar blamed Democrats for the slow roll out due to the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) shutdown earlier in the year, in the wake of the killings of Renee Good and Alex Pretti in Minneapolis. “I want the American people to see what that ICE officer sees when he takes action. And I think it’s going to help ICE with false media,” Homan added. “As of last week, they are training the trainers.” He noted that body cameras are being deployed to field offices throughout the country, and DHS has had to hire more people to handle the freedom of information requests of the body camera footage. “That’s expensive, but now we got the money that’s being implemented,” Homan said. Share Updated at 14.39 EDT 3h ago 13.41 EDT Trump speaks with newly minted British PM Andy Burnham Donald Trump has spoken to the new British prime minister, Andy Burnham, according to a read-out from Downing Street. “The prime minister began by congratulating the president on a successful World Cup and agreed that England had an excellent tournament,” according to the release. Burnham said he hopes that he has the opportunity to show Trump around Manchester, during the president’s next visit to the UK. “The prime minister also underlined his commitment to defence and security and said ensuring the security of the UK and its allies was at the top of his agenda,” Downing Street noted. “The president then updated on the situation in the Middle East, and the prime minister outlined the UK’s commitment to securing the movement of shipping in the strait of Hormuz to support global supply chains and drive down costs for businesses and families across the country.” Share Updated at 13.46 EDT 4h ago 13.18 EDT Michael Sainato High school math teacher Jake Johnson easily recalls the role government assistance played for his family in Stewartville, Minnesota, when he was growing up. His father was a garbage collector and his mother read meters for the local gas company. They relied on food stamps for groceries and Medicaid for healthcare. When it was time for Johnson to go to college, Pell grants and Stafford loans helped him pay the bill. “We were broke,” Johnson said. “I got to grow up living what I thought was an American dream, where you work hard and a lot of people invest in you.” It’s this upbringing that pushed Johnson to run for Congress, trying to take back a US House seat that hasn’t been held by a Democrat since 2018, when Tim Walz vacated it to run for governor. The congressional district is one of 44 across the country that Democrats say are “in play” in the midterms as the party seeks to gain a majority in Congress. And Johnson is one of a handful of Democratic candidates with a background in the labor movement who are trying to connect with workers frustrated with the economy. Johnson is running against incumbent Republican Brad Finstad, who has held the seat since 2022, and he is trying to convince voters that Finstad has supported policies that have hurt them. Trump’s tariffs have especially caused a surge in prices, Johnson said, in the predominantly rural district that he says is the ninth most agriculturally productive in the US. “A huge piece of affordability is ending the tariffs and rebuilding our export markets and lowering input costs for our farmers, small businesses and families,” Johnson said, adding that the Iran war has also increased fertilizer costs. “We’ve already got farmers who are planning ahead for the fall going, ‘I don’t know if I’m going to be able to get my fertilizer preordered for next season.’” High school math teacher seeks to win US House seat in Minnesota by emphasizing affordability Read more Share 4h ago 13.06 EDT Members of Congress respond to incident at 26 Federal Plaza Congressional lawmakers in New York have condemned the incident where a man allegedly set a fire outside a federal immigration building before being taken into custody. In a statement Senator Chuck Schumer, the minority leader who represents New York, said that “violence like this is never acceptable”. “I applaud local and federal law enforcement and the FDNY for their quick actions in response to the disturbing, violent attack at 26 Federal Plaza this morning. Their prompt actions kept people safe and helped apprehend the suspect,” he wrote in a statement. Similarly, New York congressman Hakeem Jeffries, the top House Democrat, called for “the individual responsible for this must be prosecuted to the fullest extent of the law”. Share 5h ago 12.23 EDT New York mayor Zohran Mamdani calls incident outside 26 Federal Plaza 'deeply disturbing' New York mayor Zohran Mamdani called the incident outside 26 Federal Plaza, which saw an “incendiary device” deployed, “deeply disturbing”. “I’m relieved no one was seriously injured and that a suspect is in custody,” Mamdani wrote on social media. “My team is in touch with the NYPD and we will support the federal investigation. Our administration will continue to ensure that every New Yorker is safe in their city – and hold accountable any who threaten that.” Share Updated at 12.37 EDT 5h ago 11.48 EDT José Olivares Ismael Zambada García, the Mexican drug lord known as “El Mayo”, who co-founded and led the Sinaloa cartel, was sentenced to life in prison by a Brooklyn federal judge on Monday morning and was ordered to forfeit $15bn in drug proceeds. The 76-year-old Zambada, who has spent nearly two years inside a Brooklyn federal prison, limped inside the courtroom dressed in a beige prison outfit, with white hair and a beard. Before his sentencing, Zambada read a public letter, saying that he took responsibility for his crimes and calling for an end to the violence that has plagued Mexico for decades during the drug-fueled conflict. “I did not go to trial because I did not want to deny the truth,” Zambada read aloud in court, adding that “violence must end” and urging the next generation of drug traffickers to “choose a different path”. Zambada helped lead the Sinaloa cartel, one of Mexico’s biggest organized crime groups, and signed a guilty plea last August admitting to trafficking tons of cocaine, marijuana, heroin and, in recent years, fentanyl. He also admitted to engaging in violence to further the cartel’s business operations and corrupting Mexican public officials. The federal judge, Brian Cogan, said his sentencing was “anticlimactic”, due to his plea agreement and the general understanding by all parties involved that Zambada would spend the rest of his life in prison. “I think he deserves life,” Cogan said, but added that even if anyone disagreed, the US government’s sentencing guidelines would require a life-in-prison sentence due to the severity of Zambada’s crimes. Zambada’s defense attorneys requested that the judge recommend the Bureau of Prisons to send Zambada to a federal medical facility, rather than a traditional prison, due to his medical conditions. The kingpin’s medical conditions are redacted in court records, but the judge mentioned during the sentencing hearing that Zambada was “drifting” towards being “cognitively impaired”. Mexican outlets have reported Zambada is dealing with the early stages of dementia. The Bureau of Prisons still has to evaluate where Zambada will be sent to serve the rest of his days. Share Updated at 11.57 EDT 5h ago 11.42 EDT FBI investigating 'incendiary device' deployed outside New York ICE holding facility The FBI is investigating an incident where an “incendiary device” that was deployed outside 26 Federal Plaza, a notorious Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) holding facility in New York. In a statement, FBI director Kash Patel said that “an individual is in custody and two minor injuries reported thus far”. The Joint Terrorism Task Force is also part of the investigation. The building is also the site of the largest federal immigration courthouse in Manhattan, where there have been several instances of people being apprehended by federal officials waiting just outside courtrooms, following immigration hearings. My colleague Julius Constantine-Motal reported from the courthouse last month, and captured instances of people being detained after their appearances in court by federal agents who are often masked. View image in fullscreen Black smoke and flames in front of 26 Federal Plaza in lower Manhattan, after an incendiary device was deployed, 20 July 2026. Photograph: Robert Sullivan/AP Share Updated at 12.11 EDT 6h ago 11.06 EDT José Olivares One of Mexico’s most important and significant drug traffickers in recent history will be sentenced this morning by a federal judge in Brooklyn and will probably spend the rest of his life behind bars. Ismael Zambada García, the 76-year-old Mexican drug lord known as “El Mayo”, helped co-found and lead the Sinaloa cartel, becoming one of the most wanted targets by US intelligence and law enforcement agencies for decades. El Mayo signed a guilty plea last August and decided not to proceed with a federal trial that would have likely lasted weeks and cost the US government millions of dollars. El Mayo is facing life-in-prison, due to his age, the gravity of his crimes and the guilty plea he signed. The US government is also asking that he pay $15bn as part of his punishment. As one of the co-founders of the Sinaloa cartel, El Mayo is one of the biggest drug traffickers in the history of the US-Mexico drug war. The US government has accused him of trafficking tons of cocaine, marijuana, heroin and, in recent years, fentanyl to the US and other parts of the world. Prosecutors also accused him of being one of the leaders of a violent organization that led to corruption among Mexican politicians, military officials and law enforcement agents. Although Joaquín “El Chapo” Guzmán is known to be the face of the Sinaloa cartel, the elderly Mayo was known to be the elusive leader, who ran the cartel’s operations from behind the scenes. El Mayo was little seen by the public and was relatively unknown, in sharp contrast to El Chapo. El Mayo’s arrest in 2024, however, came as a shock. During a mysterious operation, he was beaten and kidnapped by one of El Chapo’s sons and was flown across the border to New Mexico, where he was arrested by US federal agents. It is still unknown whether US agencies helped facilitate the cross-border kidnapping operation that led to his arrest. El Chapo’s son, who orchestrated the kidnapping, pled guilty late last year and has become a cooperating witness for the US justice department. El Mayo’s arrest led to a splintering of the Sinaloa cartel, with El Mayo’s son and El Chapo’s sons leading different factions in a raging intra-cartel war for territory that has ravaged parts of Mexico. El Mayo’s sentence comes amid a difficult time in US-Mexico relations, as the Trump administration has escalated its pressure on the Mexican government to further attack criminal groups. Last year, the Trump administration designated the Sinaloa cartel as a terrorist group and has threatened military intervention to attack the cartel and other drug trafficking organizations. Reporters are heading up to the courtroom now, where the Brooklyn federal judge Brian Cogan, who also presided over El Chapo’s trial years ago, will dictate El Mayo’s sentence. Share Updated at 11.13 EDT 6h ago 11.03 EDT As Troy Jackson becomes frontrunner for Maine Democrats, what happens next? A reminder of the next steps in the frantic replacement process in the Maine Senate race, now that Troy Jackson appears poised to clinch the Democratic nomination. Over the weekend, all 16 counties elected 500 delegates who will vote in a special convention in Bangor on Saturday – the remaining 101 delegates will come from the state Democratic party committee. This will the formal process of selecting the new nominee after Graham Platner abruptly ended his campaign following allegations of sexual assault from a former partner. Jackson, now the progressive frontrunner, once campaigned alongside Platner and received his endorsement during his gubernatorial run, where he finished third. That proximity to Platner – whose campaign was beset by controversy – raised concerns that Jackson might be tainted in the breakneck process to replace the disgraced former nominee. Questions have also lingered about Jackson’s public speaking style and whether he could match the commanding presence that helped Platner build a statewide populist following. But Jackson has consolidated support from major unions, numerous state Democrats, and national progressive groups, and he appears to have locked down broad backing from the newly selected delegates. While their commitments are not legally binding, a large majority of the 601 delegates have now pledged to vote for him. On Sunday, Jackson told Politico it was “incredible” to see people “really concerned about making sure this momentum, this movement continues” as it became clear he would emerge as the presumptive nominee and his rivals dropped out of the competition. His campaign said Monday it was turning its “full focus” to defeating Collins after the weekend’s surge of delegate support. State Democrats have until 27 July to formally choose Platner’s replacement for the general-election ballot. centerLeftText:
Washington — Sen. Darline Graham, who is serving out the remainder of the late Sen. Lindsey Graham's term in the Senate, said Monday that she will seek a full term for the South Carolina seat her brother held for more than two decades.

"I've made a decision," Graham told Fox News. "I'm in."

The late senator, who died on July 11, had secured the GOP nomination in June for what would have been his fifth term representing South Carolina. With his death, the Republican candidate in the South Carolina Senate race will be determined by a special primary election. Republicans seeking the Senate seat may file between July 21 and July 28, before an Aug. 11 primary to decide who will take on Democrat Annie Andrews in November.

Graham, 62, was sworn in last week, after South Carolina Gov. Henry McMaster appointed her to serve out the remainder of her brother's term, which runs through early January. Graham, who doesn't have a background in elected office, had a career in disabilities services and most recently served as commissioner of the South Carolina Commission for the Blind. She said upon accepting the appointment that finishing her brother's term "is what Lindsey would have wanted," noting that she planned to "honor him in this way."

President Trump publicly encouraged Graham to run for a six-year term, writing in a post on Truth Social after meeting with her at the White House last week that she would have his "Complete and Total Endorsement."

"I hope Darline does this, in that there would be nobody better to honor the legacy of her beloved brother, Lindsey," Mr. Trump said.

In an interview with Fox News' Sean Hannity, Graham said, "I have been praying a lot, talking to my family."

"I know I'm a hard worker. I learned that from Lindsey," she said. "I feel like I can do it. I feel an inner peace about it. Will it be difficult? Yes. Absolutely."

The president's endorsement hasn't stopped other Republicans from announcing their intention to run for the six-year term. Rep. Ralph Norman, who's represented South Carolina in the House since 2017, announced a bid on Saturday. And Rep. Russell Fry, who was elected to the House in 2022, announced his candidacy on Monday.

If no candidate secures a majority of the vote in the special primary election, a runoff will be held on Aug. 25.
centerText: "I've made a decision," Darline Graham told Fox News Channel host Sean Hannity , according to a clip released of an interview set to air later Monday. "I'm in." The entrance of Darline Graham — sworn in last week to fulfill the remaining months of her late brother's term, which expires in January — into the Republican scramble to select a new Senate nominee further complicates an already rushed process following Lindsey Graham 's death earlier this month. A weeklong filing period opens Tuesday for the special Republican primary, which will be Aug. 11. On Friday, President Donald Trump said that Darline Graham had his "Complete and Total Endorsement" to seek the nomination, adding, "RUN, DARLINE, RUN!" READ MORE: Who is Darline Graham, the late Sen. Lindsey Graham's sister? Last week, Gov. Henry McMaster appointed Darline Graham to serve the remainder of her brother's term, which ends in January. In his announcement, McMaster made no reference to Darline Graham as a placeholder or symbolic appointment, although a person familiar with McMaster's thinking but unauthorized to speak publicly said the governor, in selecting Darline Graham, had never contemplated that she would run for the seat herself. Before the emergence of Darline Graham — and their Oval Office meeting after she was sworn in — Trump had previously suggested he could back a potential candidacy from Rep. Russell Fry, who said Monday he would officially enter the special primary. Several other noteworthy politicians, including Rep. Ralph Norman, have already gotten in, and others like Rep. Nancy Mace and Lt. Gov. Pamela Evette have been eyeing a run. Watch the clip in the player above. Darline Graham told Hannity said she had been "praying a lot" about how to navigate her new role. "It is a tremendous amount of pressure," she said. "I know I'm a hard worker. I learned that from Lindsey," Darline Graham said. "I feel like I can do it. I feel an inner peace about it. Will it be difficult? Yes." READ MORE: Darline Graham's career centered disability. Now she could help shape national policy Funeral services for Lindsey Graham are scheduled to be held next week in Washington and South Carolina. A preliminary report from the medical examiner said he suffered a tear in his aorta. The millions in Lindsey Graham's campaign account aren't funds that Darline Graham could directly access in a run of her own, according to Bradley A. Smith, a former chairman of the Federal Election Commission. Under federal rules, Lindsey Graham's campaign would be limited to transferring just $2,000 to a potential Darline Graham candidacy. However, Smith said there is no limit on how much it could transfer to the National Republican Senatorial Committee, which could — thanks to a Supreme Court decision last month — "spend an unlimited amount in coordination with Darline's campaign."
centerRightText:
Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-SD) isn’t making an endorsement in South Carolina’s special Republican Senate contest to replace Sen. Lindsey Graham (R-SC) — at least not “at the moment.”

Still, the GOP leader on Monday lauded interim Sen. Darline Graham Nordone (R-SC), who earlier that day revealed plans to run in the upcoming special primary to replace her late brother on the November ballot, as a “strong candidate.”

“I think she obviously carries on Lindsey’s legacy, but I think she’s a very accomplished person in her own right,” Thune told reporters. “She has a right, like anybody else, to get into that race.”

farRightText:
Rep. Russell Fry (R-SC) has launched a campaign for South Carolina’s U.S. Senate seat, which was held by the late Sen. Lindsey Graham (R-SC), saying that “a conservative fighter in the Senate who delivers real results” is needed.

In a post on X, Fry expressed that “too many” residents in South Carolina “are struggling to make ends meet” as they deal with rising gas and grocery prices. Fry’s entry into the race comes after President Donald Trump gave the late Lindsey Graham’s sister, Darline Graham Nordone, his “Complete and Total Endorsement” if she decided to run in South Carolina’s special Republican primary on August 11, 2026.

Graham Nordone was sworn in as interim Senator on July 14 to finish out her brother’s term, which goes until the end of the 119th Congress on January 3, 2027.

“South Carolina, I’m in!” Fry wrote. “I’m running for the U.S. Senate. Too many South Carolina families are struggling to make ends meet. High gas prices and soaring grocery costs are hurting hardworking people.”

“We need a conservative fighter in the Senate who delivers real results,” Fry continued. “I will be your conservative MAGA candidate in the special primary Senate election this summer. I’m ready for the fight, and I’m all in.”

Other lawmakers such as Rep. Ralph Norman (R-SC) have also launched campaigns for Graham’s Senate seat.

In a press release on Monday, Fry highlighted how Graham, who died on July 11 after a “brief and sudden illness,” believed in standing with Trump “and advancing the Republican agenda.”

“There is no other way to put it: Lindsey Graham was one of a kind,” Fry said. “There is no replacing him. But I believe the best way to honor his legacy is to fight alongside the President just as steadfastly as he did.”

“Two people close to Fry” confirmed to Politico that the Republican lawmaker would “continue running for his House seat” along with being in the U.S. Senate primary race.

centerText:
US Vice-President JD Vance has announced the birth of his fourth child, a boy, with his wife Usha Vance.

Alec Neel Vance was born on Sunday morning, the vice-president said in a statement on social media.

“Usha and the baby are happy and healthy, and our kids are overjoyed to meet their little brother,” he added, also thanking the military doctors and staff at Walter Reed Medical Center for their care.

It is the first time a baby has been born to a sitting vice-president while in office for more than 150 years, according to the Associated Press. President Donald Trump hailed the news, writing in a post: "Congratulations! A perfect baby boy for the wonderful Vance family."

The pair have three other children: Ewan, 9, Vivek, 6, and four-year-old Mirabel.

Usha Vance (née Chilukuri) was born and raised in San Diego, California, to a mechanical engineer father and a molecular biologist mother who had moved to the US from Andhra Pradesh, India.

She met JD Vance as a student at Yale Law School in 2010, when they joined a discussion group on "social decline in white America".

Vice President JD Vance and his wife, Usha Vance, announced the birth of their fourth child on Sunday, making history as the first sitting vice president to welcome a child in 150 years.

The announcement welcomed the arrival of Alec Neel Vance on Sunday, the fourth sibling of Ewan, 9; Vivek, 6; and Mirabel, 4.

“We are excited to announce that our baby boy, Alec Neel Vance, was born this morning. Usha and the baby are happy and healthy, and our kids are overjoyed to meet their little brother,” the statement said.

Vance thanked the White House medical team along with doctors and staff at the Walter Reed National Military Medical Center.

As noted by the Associated Press, Vance became the first vice president since the 1800s have a child while serving as the second-highest official in the United States.

“Schuyler Colfax and his second wife, Ellen Wade, had a son, Schuyler Colfax III, in 1870 when Colfax was serving as vice president, according to the White House Historical Association,” it noted. “Decades before that, John C. Calhoun and his wife, Floride Bonneau, had a son, William, in 1829 when Calhoun was vice president, the association said.”

Colfax served under President Ulysses S. Grant between 1869-1873.

Several members of the Trump administration have welcomed babies during the president’s second term, including White House deputy chief of staff Stephen Miller and White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt.

topic: Sentenced Life Prison Mayo Cartel Drug

left.title: Mexican drug lord ‘El Mayo’ sentenced to life in prison in US federal court

left-center.title: Infamous cartel boss "El Mayo" Zambada sentenced to life in U.S. prison

center.title: Notorious drug cartel leader Ismael Zambada sentenced to life in US prison

right.title: Sinaloa Cartel Kingpin 'El Mayo' Sentenced in U.S. to Life in Prison

farLeftText: The Mexican drug kingpin, Ismael Zambada García, known as “El Mayo”, was sentenced in a US federal court on Monday to life in prison, after a plea deal last year in which he admitted ⁠to shipping vast amounts of cocaine and other substances as leader of the violent Sinaloa cartel. Zambada is 76 and one of the most high-profile Mexican ⁠drug traffickers to be sentenced in a US court since his fellow Sinaloa leader Joaquín “El Chapo” Guzmán ​was sentenced to life in prison in ‌2019. The case unfolded in federal court in Brooklyn, New York. Zambada helped co-found and lead the cartel, becoming one of the most wanted targets by US intelligence and law enforcement agencies for decades. He signed a plea deal last August, admitting charges including racketeering conspiracy and continuing criminal enterprise, after the ‌US justice department said it would not seek the death penalty. The court also ordered him to forfeit $15bn from drug trade profits as part of his punishment. As one of the co-founders of the Sinaloa cartel, El Mayo is one of the biggest drug traffickers in the history of the US-Mexico drug war. He was accused of trafficking tons of cocaine, marijuana, heroin and, in recent years, fentanyl to the US and other parts of the world. Prosecutors also accused him of being one of the leaders of a violent organization that led to corruption among Mexican politicians, military officials and law enforcement agents. Zambada, who has spent nearly two years inside a Brooklyn federal prison, limped inside the courtroom. He was wearing a beige prison outfit and had white hair and a beard. Before his sentencing, Zambada read a public letter, saying that he took responsibility for his crimes and calling for an end to the violence that has plagued Mexico for decades during the drug-fueled conflict. “I did not go to trial because I did not want to deny the truth,” Zambada read aloud in court, adding that “violence must end” and urging the next generation of drug traffickers to “choose a different path”. While El Chapo is better known as the face of the Sinaloa cartel, the elderly El Mayo was regarded as the elusive, behind the scenes leader running the operations, with a low profile and few public sightings. El Mayo’s arrest in the US in 2024 came as a surprise. During a mysterious operation, he was known to have been beaten and kidnapped by one of El Chapo’s sons, Joaquín Guzmán López, and was flown across the US border to New Mexico, where he was arrested by US federal agents. It remains unknown whether US agencies helped facilitate the cross-border kidnapping operation that led to his arrest. Guzmán López pled guilty in an abduction case late last year and has become a cooperating witness for the US justice department. El Mayo’s arrest led to a splintering of the Sinaloa cartel, with El Mayo’s son and El Chapo’s sons leading different factions, in a raging intra-cartel battle for territory that has ravaged parts of Mexico. El Mayo’s sentencing comes at a tense time in US-Mexico relations, as the Trump administration has escalated its pressure on the Mexican government to crack down harder on criminal groups. Last year, the Trump administration designated the Sinaloa cartel as a terrorist group and has threatened US military intervention to attack the cartel and other drug-trafficking organizations. US district judge Brian Cogan, who also presided over El Chapo’s trial, has been hearing the case in New York. Cogan acknowledged that the sentencing hearing was “anticlimactic” because of the plea agreement. All parties involved understood that Zambada would spend the rest of his life in prison, the judge said. Frank Perez, a lawyer for El Mayo, ​had said in a 6 July court filing that his client was fully aware he would receive a life sentence when he decided to accept responsibility for his crimes. Perez asked Cogan to recommend to the US Bureau of Prisons that his client, as a septuagenarian, be placed in a ⁠facility where he would receive adequate medical care. Prosecutors with the Brooklyn US attorney’s office said the prisons ​suggested by Perez may not ​be equipped to handle the security concerns posed by Zambada, including the ​possibility that he could ‌continue to direct ​cartel activities from ​behind bars. “Those security concerns will continue for the foreseeable future because individuals loyal to the defendant continue to control the cartel,” the prosecutors wrote in a 13 July filing. Zambada’s defense attorneys on Monday requested that the judge recommend a federal medical facility, rather than a traditional prison, due to his medical conditions. Health details are redacted in court records, but the judge mentioned during the sentencing hearing that Zambada is “drifting” towards being “cognitively impaired”. Mexican outlets have reported Zambada is dealing with the early stages of dementia. The Bureau of Prisons still has to evaluate where Zambada will be sent to serve the rest of his days. El Chapo is serving his life sentence at a maximum-security prison in Colorado. Frank Perez, one of Zambada’s attorneys, said during the sentencing hearing that his client did not cooperate with the US government, neither providing information to US officials about other cartel members or leaders, nor Mexican officials who have protected the cartel’s drug-trafficking business. Reuters contributed reporting
centerLeftText: Former Mexican drug kingpin Ismael "El Mayo" Zambada was sentenced in a U.S. court Monday to life in prison, seven years after his Sinaloa cartel co-founder Joaquín "El Chapo" Guzmán was handed the same sentence. Zambada's sentencing in Brooklyn federal court was the culmination of the U.S. government's yearslong fight to bring the long-elusive cartel leader to justice. He was arrested in 2024 after he said he was kidnapped in Mexico and flown to Texas. "No one wins in a war like this," the 76-year-old Zambada said through a Spanish-language translator in court. He apologized "for the harm I caused and for the example I set." He urged future generations to "choose a different path." "If there's one thing I can say that has value today, it's this: the violence must end," the Sinaloa cartel co-founder said. "In Mexico and elsewhere affected by this type of crime, too many lives are lost, too many families are destroyed, and too many young people are trapped in a cycle that leads only to prison or death." Zambada ordered torture and plotted murders as he and co-founder Joaquín "El Chapo" Guzmán grew the Sinaloa cartel into the world's largest drug trafficking organization, federal prosecutors said. In indictments against Zambada in both New York and Texas, prosecutors alleged that Zambada and other Sinaloa cartel leaders were responsible for massive quantities of heroin, cocaine and fentanyl and other illegal drugs being distributed into the U.S. A Justice Department statement said U.S. District Judge Brian Cogan sentenced him to life in prison in the federal court in Brooklyn without the possibility of parole for his role as a principal leader of a "continuing criminal enterprise" and for racketeering violations. "Ismael Zambada Garcia spent nearly four decades poisoning American communities to make billions of dollars in profit and ordering the murders of anyone who stood in his way. Today, that chapter closes for good," U.S. Attorney for the Eastern District of New York Joseph Nocella, Jr. said in a statement. Zambada was seen as the cartel's strategist and dealmaker and was more involved in its day-to-day operations than the flamboyant Guzmán, who was convicted in 2019 and is serving a life sentence at the federal Supermax prison in Florence, Colorado. Zambada pleaded guilty in August 2025 after prosecutors said they wouldn't seek the death penalty. Speaking through a Spanish-language interpreter, he apologized "to everyone who has suffered or been affected by my actions." The 76-year-old Zambada has asked that he serve his sentence at a prison hospital. Zambada "contends with a complex of age-related health issues" and "will need ongoing access to medical care," defense lawyer Frank Perez said in a sentencing memo. A photo of ormer Sinaloa Cartel leader Ismael "El Mayo" Zambada being detained by federal agents is displayed next to a Beechcraft King Air aircraft in Santa Teresa, New Mexico, July 9, 2026. Paul Ratje/REUTERS Federal prosecutors asked Judge Brian M. Cogan to consider "the serious ongoing security risks" Zambada poses, arguing that some facilities "may not be appropriate for the security concerns posed." Judges often make recommendations about where inmates should serve time, but it's up to the federal Bureau of Prisons to decide. Zambada "was one of the most-if not the most-prolific and powerful narcotraffickers in the world," prosecutors said in a sentencing memo. "It would be difficult to overstate the magnitude" of his crimes and the scale of the "corruption, violence, and other harm he spread throughout Mexico, the United States, and the world." Along with a life sentence, he also faces $15 billion in financial penalties, which prosecutors said reflected proceeds from the cartel's crimes under his leadership. Zambada pleaded guilty to charges of engaging in a continuing criminal enterprise and racketeering conspiracy, acknowledging culpability for 85 underlying crimes. From 1980 until last year, he and his cartel were responsible for transporting at least 1.5 million kilograms of cocaine, "most of which went to the United States," he said upon pleading guilty. He acknowledged the scope of the Sinaloa operation, including underlings who built relationships with cocaine producers in Colombia, oversaw importing cocaine to Mexico by boat and plane and smuggling the drug across the U.S.-Mexico border. He said the cartel raked in hundreds of millions of dollars a year and admitted that people working for him paid hefty bribes to Mexican officials "so they could operate freely." "Systematic assassinations" Months before his arrest, prosecutors said, Zambada ordered the murder of his nephew Eliseo Imperial Castro, also known as "Cheyo Antrax," after learning that Castro was claiming to be collecting debts for Zambada and pocketing the money without Zambada's permission. In 2023, at least three people were killed in retaliatory violence that Zambada directed after a large cache of fentanyl pills, methamphetamine and cocaine was stolen from the cartel, prosecutors said. According to U.S. prosecutors, Zambada also engaged in "systematic assassinations" of fellow cartel members, police and the military. In 2010, he gave a rare interview to the Mexican magazine Proceso, revealing that the Mexican army had come close to capturing him four times. Known as the "man in the hat," Zambada said he feared jail more than death, and his arrest or murder would not change Mexico's drug trafficking landscape. Zambada's arrest came after, according to his lawyer, he was beaten, tied up, abducted and driven to a private plane by one of Guzmán's sons, Joaquín Guzmán López. Their arrests touched off deadly fighting in Sinaloa that pitted Zambada's loyalists against backers of Guzmán's sons, dubbed the Chapitos, or "little Chapos." According to an indictment by the U.S. Justice Department, the Chapitos and their cartel associates have used corkscrews, electrocution and hot chiles to torture their rivals while some of their victims were "fed dead or alive to tigers." In arguing to have Zambada sent to a prison hospital, Perez sought to contrast Zambada's guilty plea and Guzmán's decision to go to trial — an 11-week ordeal that involved closing down the Brooklyn Bridge to shuttle him to and from court and jail. "Mr. Zambada's unequivocal acceptance of responsibility has been a defining feature of his conduct since his involuntary arrival in the United States," Perez wrote. "For Mr. Zambada to end up not only with the same life sentence as Mr. Guzman, but also in the same draconian Supermax prison, would fail to account in any way for the fact that he took the right path in resolving the charges against him," Perez argued. Earlier this month, Mexico said it was investigating whether its sovereignty was violated by the U.S. when Zambada was captured after the FBI displayed in an exhibition the plane used to bring Zambada to the U.S. The U.S. Embassy in Mexico had previously said no U.S. agency had participated in the operation, Mexican government secretary Rosa Icela Rodriguez said. "The versions are contradictory. Someone lied," she said.
centerText: One of the most notorious figures in Mexico's drug cartels, Ismael "El Mayo" Zambada, will spend the rest of his life in prison, a US judge has ruled, two years after the kingpin was lured to the US in an elaborate sting operation. The 76-year-old, who founded the Sinaloa Cartel with Joaquin "El Chapo" Guzmán, apologised in court and was also ordered to pay $15bn (£11bn). In 2024, El Chapo's son led Zambada to believe they were travelling to northern Mexico to inspect clandestine airstrips, when their plane instead landed in Texas and he was arrested. Last year, Zambada reached a plea deal and admitted to drug smuggling and conspiracy charges in exchange for avoiding the death penalty. On Monday, US District Judge Brian Cogan sentenced Zambada during a hearing in Brooklyn federal court. His guilty plea carried a mandatory term of life in prison. Zambada spoke through a Spanish translator during the hearing. He said he was sorry "for the harm I caused and for the example I set" and called for future generations to "choose a different path". "If there's one thing I can say that has value today, it's this: the violence must end," said the former cartel boss. "In Mexico and elsewhere affected by this type of crime, too many lives are lost, too many families are destroyed, and too many young people are trapped in a cycle that leads only to prison or death." The $15bn he was ordered to pay represents drug-trafficking profits from his role as the principal leader of a criminal enterprise, according to the justice department. El Chapo's son, Joaquin Guzmán Lopez, was also arrested in July 2024 and has pleaded guilty to federal drug charges. He has yet to be sentenced. In the plea deal, Zambada officially accepted his role in creating the vast criminal network which has sent huge amounts of cocaine and other drugs into the US since he co-founded the Sinaloa Cartel at the end of the 1980s. "Ismael Zambada Garcia spent nearly four decades poisoning American communities to make billions of dollars in profit and ordering the murders of anyone who stood in his way," said US Attorney Joseph Nocella Jr for the Eastern District of New York in a statement. "Today, that chapter closes for good." Prosecutors argued in a sentencing memo that Zambada "was one of the most-if not the most-prolific and powerful narcotraffickers in the world", according to CBS, the BBC's US partner. The Sinaloa Cartel has been at war with itself since Zambada was taken into custody and has wreaked mayhem across the Mexican state of Sinaloa. Hundreds of people have been reported missing during fighting between its two rival factions. Violence and bloodletting also shook the cartel after El Chapo was arrested and extradited to the US in 2016 as factions vied for control of territory while fighting off opposing drug gangs who sensed weakness. He is also serving life in prison. One of the issues that has arisen since Zambada agreed to plead guilty has been where he will serve his sentence. He has asked to be sent to a prison hospital, saying he will need medical care for age-related health issues. He is understood to have been in poor health in recent years. Federal prosecutors, though, are seeking a higher-level of incarceration, contending Zambada still poses a security threat while behind bars and could continue to direct illegal drug operations. The federal Bureau of Prisons will make the final decision about where he is held, though he looks almost certain to die in US custody. On his first day back in office, US President Donald Trump announced he would increase pressure on Mexico and the cartels, including designating the criminal enterprises terrorist groups. The US also accused the governor of Sinaloa of conspiring with drug traffickers. The Mexican government, too, has taken more aggressive steps against cartels and sent thousands of troops to Sinaloa. The question of what Zambada's punishment means to the Sinaloa cartel is unclear. In all probability, it will not change the status quo. Organised crime moves on quickly in Mexico and El Mayo's former footsoldiers have already adapted to life without him. Neither is it likely to significantly affect the traffic of cocaine and fentanyl into the US. There is always another ready to take over the mantle, even from so influential a crime boss as El Mayo Zambada.

farRightText: The maximum leader of Mexico’s most powerful drug cartel, the terrorist organization known as the Sinaloa Cartel, will spend the rest of his life in prison. The kingpin and his organization are linked directly to the ongoing fentanyl crisis that has been plaguing the United States On Monday morning, Ismael “El Mayo” Zambada went before U.S. District Judge Brian Cogan, who handed down the life sentence. The sentence was anticipated ahead of time following his August 2025 guilty plea, where the Mexican drug lord admitted to multiple drug trafficking conspiracy charges linked to his role as one of the founders and top leaders of the Sinaloa Cartel. According to the U.S. Department of Justice, the Sinaloa Cartel is responsible for moving tons of cocaine, methamphetamine, and fentanyl into the United States. Additionally, the criminal organization has a long history of extreme violence being directly linked to numerous murders, mass killings, forced disappearances, and other high-level crimes. As Breitbart Texas has reported, Zambada’s arrest sparked a political storm and a fierce turf war that split the Sinaloa Cartel in two. During the summer of 2024, members of the Los Chapitos faction of the Sinaloa Cartel called a meeting with El Mayo and some high-level politicians only to kidnap the drug lord and fly him to the United States and turn him over to authorities. The operation fueled tensions between the United States and Mexico. Since the start of the Trump Administration, the U.S. government has been pressuring Mexico to fight drug cartels. Earlier this year, tensions between the two countries escalated when the U.S. Department of Justice criminally indicted the current governor of Sinaloa, Rubén Rocha Moya, and nine of his closest allies for allegedly working with the Sinaloa Cartel. Rather than assist the U.S. with arrests and extradition, Sheinbaum has publicly defended Rocha Moya, claiming that there is no proof of wrongdoing against the politician. farLeftText: Social media influencers Andrew and Tristan Tate will fight extradition to the United Kingdom, their lawyer told a US magistrate in Miami on Monday after the brothers were arrested to face rape and sex-trafficking charges. The Tates appeared in federal court for the first time since their arrest on Saturday and are being held in federal detention. In the coming weeks, a US district judge will consider whether they meet the conditions for extradition. The brothers, normally photographed in tailored suits and stylish fitted shirts, wore tan jail uniforms and handcuffs during the brief hearing. Joseph McBride, an attorney for the brothers, has decried the charges as “filth and slander”. On Sunday, McBride said he was confident the extradition request would be denied. US magistrate Lauren Louis set another hearing for next Monday. Prosecutors in Britain said the new charges relate to four new victims and were brought after authorities received evidence from Bedfordshire police in south-eastern England. The allegations, spanning from 2010 to 2017, include rape, assault, trafficking and offenses relating to “indecent images of a child and extreme pornography”. The brothers are already charged with rape, bodily harm, human trafficking and controlling prostitution for gain relating to three other alleged victims in the UK between 2012 and 2015. Their lawyer said in a statement on Saturday that he believed the arrests were “greenlighted by a low-level functionary” at the justice department without input from leadership. The justice department told the Associated Press that the arrests were approved by leadership of its criminal division. The new allegations are the latest in a long-running international legal saga involving the Tates that has spanned the US, Britain and Romania. The brothers have repeatedly denied wrongdoing. Their social media empire promoting male dominance, misogyny and wealth has made them among the world’s most polarizing internet personalities. The dual US and British citizens moved to Romania in 2016. They were arrested there in 2022, accused of participating in schemes to lure women for sexual exploitation. They denied those allegations and the Romanian case has not gone forward because of legal and procedural problems. Last year, they were allowed to leave Romania and flew to Florida.
centerLeftText:
The influencer brothers Andrew and Tristan Tate will remain in custody as their lawyers told a court on Monday that they plan to fight extradition to England to face new charges of rape and sex trafficking.

The Tates appeared in federal court after marshals arrested them on Saturday at a bare-knuckle boxing event in downtown Miami. The two wore tan jail jumpsuits and shackles, speaking only to acknowledge that they had read court documents pertaining to their case.

Their attorney, Joseph McBride, told reporters after the hearing that the charges were politically motivated. “Andrew and Tristan are innocent,” he said, calling the allegations against them “garbage.”

The Tates have hired the high-profile South Florida law firm of Roy Black to fight the extradition. The attorneys will convene before a magistrate judge in one week to discuss scheduling a hearing to decide whether the brothers will remain in detention while they fight extradition.

Andrew Tate and his brother, Tristan Tate, were arrested Saturday in Miami after U.S. Marshals took the controversial social media influencers into custody on a sealed warrant as British prosecutors announced dozens of new criminal charges and sought their extradition. Bedfordshire Police also confirmed the brothers were arrested Saturday in Miami in connection with a U.K. police investigation into alleged sexual offenses dating back to 2012. Photos and video published by TMZ appeared to show federal agents handcuffing the brothers outside the James L. 'AMERICA'S MOST WANTED' FUGITIVE CAPTURED ON SAILBOAT OFF NJ COAST AFTER 20 YEARS ON THE RUN The arrests came hours after Britain's Crown Prosecution Service announced it had approved a major expansion of the criminal case against the brothers. centerRightText: NEW You can now listen to Fox News articles! As the field for the race to replace the late Sen. Lindsey Graham takes shape, one prominent lawmaker is declining to dive into the arena hours after the contest was shaken up. Rep. Nancy Mace, R-S.C., opted against a bid after she strongly suggested that she’d throw her hat into the arena on Monday in a video statement. Last August, Mace launched a campaign for the Republican gubernatorial nomination in the race to succeed term-limited GOP Gov. Henry McMaster, but she finished a disappointing fifth out of five major candidates in last month’s Republican primary. "I want to be straight with you, I'm not running for the U.S. Senate, not because I'm backing down from a fight, but because the one I'm already in matters right now," Mace said. "I'm not chasing another title, I'm chasing justice, justice for me, justice for other women and justice for every little girl who was told to stay silent," she continued. "That's where my focus is today, and that's where it's going to stay." FOX NEWS CHANNEL’S 'HANNITY' TO AIR EXCLUSIVE FIRST INTERVIEW WITH SEN DARLINE GRAHAM ON MONDAY Her decision followed a move by Sen. Darline Graham, R-S.C., to take up her brother's mantle after she was appointed to finish off the remainder of her late brother's term. She made her decision to officially enter the upcoming special election after a push from President Donald Trump. Graham, during an interview with Fox News’ Sean Hannity, acknowledged the "tremendous amount of pressure" to take over the position and run. "I feel like I can do it, I feel an inner peace about it," Graham said. "Will it be difficult? Yes." When asked if she had made a decision on what she would do, she told Hannity she had. "I'm in," Graham said. Her addition to the growing roster of hopefuls to replace the late lawmaker will likely shake up the race less than one day before candidates can begin officially filing to run for the nomination. Graham will come up against other challengers in her bid to claim the GOP nomination. Reps. Ralph Norman, R-S.C., Russell Fry, R-S.C., and businessman Mark Lynch, have all publicly committed to running. And as the filing deadline fast approaches, both Mace and Norman turned their steel against their colleague, Fry, in what’s shaping up to be a messy, Palmetto State brawl. Mace argued that should Fry win the nomination, and subsequently the general election, he would vacate his "Republican House seat until next May." REPUBLICANS DEFY TRUMP, JUMP INTO SOUTH CAROLINA SENATE RACE DESPITE HIS HANDPICKED SUCCESSOR "That narrows an already razor-thin majority and puts the Speaker's gavel in play," Mace said. "I will never hand Hakeem Jeffries that gift." "If you're running for two offices at once, you've already told us who you are," she continued. "That's not about politics anymore — that's about power. You're either with the people you serve or you're not." Fry announced Monday morning that he would enter the race, hours after Norman tossed in his hat, too. Mace has been making public overtures that she wants to jump in, but has so far not made anything official. A source in Fry’s political orbit told Fox News Digital that the lawmaker has "accomplished more than all the potential candidates combined," having passed three bills in his first four years in Congress. "This is really about giving voters a choice and not a coronation," they said. Still, the conservative duo are going for blood against Fry, arguing that his bid for the seat is purely politically motivated and jeopardizes the House’s already slim majority heading into the midterm elections later this year. Under South Carolina law, Fry can appear for multiple offices on the ballot. He was unopposed for his primary election for South Carolina’s 7th congressional district, and if he wins the nomination, he’ll be on the ballot twice for both. TRUMP REVEALS WHO HE'S EYEING TO REPLACE LINDSEY GRAHAM Ultimately he’ll have to choose if he wins both, which would trigger another special election to fill his place. The top two Republicans in the South Carolina Statehouse, South Carolina Senate President Thomas Alexander and South Carolina House Speaker Murrell Smith, said in a statement that the upcoming special election has "highlighted issues in South Carolina law," and noted that, "In the coming weeks, we intend to address these important issues so that we can provide the process available for our citizens." CLICK HERE TO DOWNLOAD THE FOX NEWS APP That is not a problem for either Mace nor Norman, who both ran and failed to clinch the Republican gubernatorial nomination last month. "SC Republican voters soundly rejected Nancy and Ralph as recently as last month because conservatives are tired of career politicians who care more about their own social media than getting actual results for families," South Carolina state Sen. JD Chaplin, and a spokesperson for Fry's campaign, told Fox News Digital. Norman was less venomous in his take on the situation, and noted on X that he respected Fry and had enjoyed his time serving with him in the House. Still, his candidacy, he argued, "could leave his seat vacant until May 18th." "This would leave his constituents unrepresented, jeopardize President Trump’s agenda, and put the House at risk of being led by a Speaker Hakeem Jeffries," Norman said. "We can’t let that happen!!"

topic: Maine Replace Graham Platner Progressive Frontrunner

left.title: Progressive frontrunner and Maine logger poised to replace Graham Platner in chaotic primary

left-center.title: Maine Democrat Shenna Bellows drops out of race to replace Graham Platner

center.title: Maine Democrats close in on pick to replace Senate candidate Graham Platner

farLeftText: Troy Jackson – a logger, longtime legislator and former president of the Maine senate – is now poised to become the Democratic nominee in Maine’s US Senate race. His rise comes after the party’s previous nominee, Graham Platner, abruptly ended his campaign earlier this month following allegations of sexual assault from a former partner. Platner’s withdrawal set off a frantic scramble inside the Maine Democratic party. Several contenders filed paperwork to be considered at a 25 July special convention of 601 delegates tasked with selecting a new nominee. But over the weekend, Jackson’s serious challengers dropped out one after another, clearing a swift and unobstructed path for him. Jackson, who lost the Democratic gubernatorial primary earlier this year, is now the presumptive choice. Among those who ended their bids was Jordan Wood, a former congressional staffer who ran unsuccessfully in the Democratic primary for Maine’s second district. Two other candidates who also fell short in this year’s gubernatorial primary – former Maine CDC director Nirav Shah and current secretary of state Shenna Bellows – likewise ended their campaigns. Dan Kleban, founder of Maine Beer Company, who briefly ran in the Senate primary against Platner before withdrawing months ago, also announced he would not stay in the race. Wood, Shah and Kleban have all endorsed Jackson, stressing the stakes of flipping the seat held for nearly three decades by Republican Susan Collins – the only GOP senator running for re-election in a state Kamala Harris carried in 2024. “This fight is bigger than any one person,” Wood said in a social media video. “There is too much at stake in our country, our democracy – our state is depending on us.” Maine is crucial to Democrats’ effort to net the four seats required to retake control of the Senate in November’s midterms. Over the weekend, all 16 counties elected 500 delegates who will vote in Bangor on Saturday, with the remaining 101 delegates coming from the state party’s central committee. Jackson, now the progressive frontrunner, once campaigned alongside Platner and received his endorsement during his gubernatorial run, where he finished third, which raised concerns that Jackson might be tainted in the breakneck process to replace the disgraced former nominee. Questions have also lingered about Jackson’s public speaking style and whether he could match the commanding presence that helped Platner build a statewide following for his populist platform. But Jackson has consolidated support from major unions, numerous state Democrats and national progressive groups – he also appears to have locked down broad backing from the newly selected delegates. While their commitments are not legally binding, a majority of the 601 delegates have now pledged to vote for him, according to Jackson’s campaign. “Those delegates were not handed to us. You know, we earned them because hundreds and hundreds of people organized their neighbors, made the calls, had the conversations,” Jackson said in Sanford. On Sunday, Jackson also told Politico it was “incredible” to see people “really concerned about making sure this momentum, this movement continues” as it became clear he would emerge as the presumptive nominee. His campaign said on Monday it was turning its “full focus” to defeating Collins after the weekend’s surge of delegate support. State Democrats have until 27 July to formally choose Platner’s replacement for the general election ballot. Jackson, 58, was first elected to the legislature in 2002 and became senate president in 2018. He left office in 2024 due to term limits.

centerText: Troy Jackson, a former Maine lawmaker and fifth-generation logger from the North Maine Woods, has emerged as the likely new replacement for the state's Democratic Senate nominee after former candidate Graham Platner quit the high-stakes race less than two weeks ago. Platner's departure followed a sexual assault allegation that he denied but forced his exit anyway. It sparked a wave of potential candidates eager to prove to Mainers that they could take on longtime Republican Sen. Susan Collins. But by Sunday, the field had largely been cleared after Jackson supporters spent the weekend securing hundreds of delegates who promised to back him at the upcoming state convention. Under a never-before-used system to replace Platner, delegates will vote Saturday in Bangor on a new Senate nominee. Out of the 601 delegates who will gather this weekend, Jackson's campaign says they have 481 delegates committed to supporting him. Top opponents — ranging from Secretary of State Shenna Bellows to Nirav Shah, former director of Maine's Center for Disease Control and Prevention — have since dropped out, while Jackson announced Monday that he was focusing his attention on ousting Collins. "I'm grateful to Democrats across Maine for putting their faith in me and in what we're fighting for," Jackson said in a statement. "This didn't happen because of one person. It happened because hundreds of volunteers put in the hours, knocked on doors, made phone calls, and believed we could build something bigger than ourselves." There are challenges ahead for Jackson in a race with Collins Yet securing the nomination is only just the beginning of challenges facing Jackson. There's little more than 100 days until the general election, meaning Jackson faces an uphill battle raising campaign funds when Collins already has millions. Jackson also must implement a statewide campaign network and woo over the state's large independent base. As former state Senate president, Jackson may be more politically vetted than Platner was running as a first-time candidate, but the logger's backing of progressive policies will leave him open to plenty of attacks by Republicans, said Nicholas Jacobs, a political scientist at Colby College in Maine. "The guy does have some pretty interesting positions," Jacobs said. "Positions that are pretty far to the left of the median voter." READ MORE: Maine Democrats criticize GOP Sen. Susan Collins after fatal ICE shooting of motorist in the state Jackson supports 'Medicare for All' and eliminating ICE Like Platner, Jackson has said he wants to "get rid of" Immigration and Customs Enforcement, which has become top of mind for many Mainers after an ICE officer fatally shot a driver last week. Democrats have rushed to connect Collins to the embattled federal agency, while Jackson has reiterated his support of dissolving ICE altogether. Jackson has also called for "Medicare for All," which would replace job-based and individual private health insurance with a government-run plan that guarantees coverage for all with no premiums, no deductibles and only minimal copays for certain services. Republicans were already criticizing Jackson as a "socialist" on Monday. "This entire process is just as bad as Kamala's rigged coronation," said Republican National Committee spokesperson Kristen Cianci, nodding to former President Joe Biden's decision to drop out of the presidential race in July 2024, leaving Vice President Kamala Harris to head up the Democratic ticket and ultimately lose to Republican Donald Trump. "Maine voters have had their voices stolen by party insiders," Cianci added. Jackson first ran for office as a Republican However, unlike the scrutiny Platner faced from a series of controversies, Jackson's background coming from "old Maine" and his decades of working in the logging industry will help him, Jacobs said. "He's the real deal," Jacobs said. In 1998, Jackson helped lead a weeklong blockade along the Quebec border in an effort to protest the hiring of Canadian loggers who were coming into Maine and driving down pay rates. The standoff ended with Jackson being escorted away by police, but resulted in Jackson fighting for better logger and wood hauler protections as a state lawmaker. Jackson initially ran as a Republican in 2000 when he sought a seat inside Maine's House of Representatives. He lost, but won two years later when he ran as an independent. He switched to the Democratic Party in 2004, and eventually became the Maine Senate president before leaving the Statehouse in 2024. He came in third while running for governor in this year's Democratic primary election, but had the backing of Platner, U.S. Sen. Bernie Sanders and Our Revolution, the political organization founded by Sanders. Democrats must net four Senate seats to gain control of the 100-member chamber, and party leaders viewed Maine as a critical piece of the puzzle, along with Alaska, Ohio and North Carolina.

“The pain is immense and it will take time for this wound to heal,” Messi wrote in Spanish on social media.
